## Binary Search Tree Insertion — Time Complexity

**Average case:** O(log n)
**Worst case:** O(n) — occurs when the tree degenerates into a linked list (e.g., inserting sorted data).

### BST Insertion Pseudocode

```
function insert(root, key):
    if root is null:
        return new Node(key)
    if key < root.key:
        root.left = insert(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key:
        root.right = insert(root.right, key)
    return root
```

The depth of the tree determines cost. An unbalanced BST can reach depth *n*, hence the O(n) worst case.

---

## How a Red-Black Tree Maintains Balance

A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST that enforces **five invariants** to guarantee O(log n) height:

| # | Invariant |
|---|-----------|
| 1 | Every node is either **red** or **black**. |
| 2 | The **root** is always black. |
| 3 | Every **null leaf** (NIL) is black. |
| 4 | A **red node** cannot have a red child (no two consecutive reds). |
| 5 | Every path from a node to its descendant NIL leaves has the **same number of black nodes** (black-height). |

These properties guarantee that the longest path is at most **2× the shortest path**, keeping height ≤ 2·log₂(n+1).

### Insertion with Rebalancing — Pseudocode

```
function rb_insert(tree, key):
    node = bst_insert(tree, key)      // Standard BST insert
    node.color = RED                   // New nodes always start red
    rb_insert_fixup(tree, node)        // Restore invariants

function rb_insert_fixup(tree, z):
    while z.parent.color == RED:
        if z.parent == z.parent.parent.left:
            uncle = z.parent.parent.right

            // ── Case 1: Uncle is RED ──
            // Recolor parent, uncle, and grandparent.
            // Push the "violation" up the tree.
            if uncle.color == RED:
                z.parent.color      = BLACK
                uncle.color         = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                z = z.parent.parent            // move z up

            else:
                // ── Case 2: Uncle is BLACK, z is a right child ──
                // Left-rotate to convert to Case 3.
                if z == z.parent.right:
                    z = z.parent
                    left_rotate(tree, z)

                // ── Case 3: Uncle is BLACK, z is a left child ──
                // Recolor and right-rotate grandparent.
                z.parent.color        = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                right_rotate(tree, z.parent.parent)

        else:
            // Mirror: swap "left" and "right" in all of the above.
            ... (symmetric cases)

    tree.root.color = BLACK            // Ensure root is always black
```

### The Three Fix-Up Cases (Illustrated)

```
Case 1: Recolor          Case 2: Rotate to Case 3    Case 3: Rotate + Recolor

      G(B)                      G(B)                       P(B)
     / \                       / \                        / \
   P(R)  U(R)    →          P(R)  U(B)    →           Z(R)  G(R)
   /                          \                                \
 Z(R)                        Z(R)                             U(B)

Recolor P,U,G            left_rotate(P)           right_rotate(G)
move Z up to G                                    recolor P,G
```

### Why It Works

- **Case 1** pushes the red-red violation upward without rotations, potentially resolving it at a higher level or at the root (which gets forced to black).
- **Cases 2 & 3** perform at most **2 rotations** to fix the local violation permanently.
- Since Case 1 moves up the tree and Cases 2/3 terminate, the fix-up runs in **O(log n)** time with at most **2 rotations** per insertion.

### Rotation Pseudocode

```
function left_rotate(tree, x):
    y = x.right
    x.right = y.left           // turn y's left subtree into x's right
    if y.left ≠ NIL:
        y.left.parent = x
    y.parent = x.parent        // link x's parent to y
    if x.parent == NIL:
        tree.root = y
    else if x == x.parent.left:
        x.parent.left = y
    else:
        x.parent.right = y
    y.left = x                 // put x on y's left
    x.parent = y
```

`right_rotate` is the symmetric mirror.

### Summary

| Operation | BST (worst) | Red-Black Tree (worst) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| Insert    | O(n)        | **O(log n)**         |
| Search    | O(n)        | **O(log n)**         |
| Delete    | O(n)        | **O(log n)**         |
| Rotations per insert | — | **≤ 2**          |

The red-black tree trades a small constant-factor overhead (color tracking, occasional rotations) for a **guaranteed logarithmic height**, making it the backbone of many standard library implementations (`std::map` in C++, `TreeMap` in Java, etc.).
